sectionName: Chemical & Physical Foundations of Biological Systems

questions.passage:
Researchers investigated a cooling patch that removed heat from a skin model by evaporating water from a hydrogel surface to connect a measurable physical trend with a the physical sciences principle. The central idea was that vaporization requires energy, so evaporation can cool the remaining material by absorbing heat. Before collecting data, the team identified the governing relationship as q = mLv for phase change at constant temperature during vaporization. This mattered because the same topic vocabulary could support several tempting but incompatible answers unless the variables, units, and controlled conditions were kept straight. The investigators therefore designed the experiment so that the figure had to be interpreted through proportionality, limiting behavior, and the direction of causality rather than through sentence recall alone.

The experimental manipulation involved increasing the mass of water evaporated while measuring heat removed from the skin model. Figure 1 summarizes the main result: heat removed increased approximately in proportion to evaporated mass. The investigators repeated the measurements after verifying that airflow, initial temperature, patch area, and humidity were controlled. Those controls were important because a change in a calibration setting, environmental condition, sample geometry, or hidden concentration could create an apparent trend without testing the intended physical relationship. A correct explanation must therefore account for both what was deliberately changed and what was explicitly held constant.

Two alternative interpretations were considered. One was that evaporation releases heat into the skin model because vapor is less dense than liquid. Another was that phase changes cannot transfer heat unless temperature changes at the same time. Each alternative contains a familiar phrase from chemistry or physics, so it could attract a student who recognizes a term but does not check whether the prediction matches the graph. The investigators rejected these explanations because they either reverse the expected direction, treat a nonlinear relationship as linear, ignore a conserved quantity, or attribute the effect to a controlled variable.

The follow-up analysis asked students to make a prediction from the same relationship. For this passage, the key is to connect the measured trend to evaporative cooling patch, translate the graph into the appropriate mathematical dependency, and use the controls to rule out artifacts. The relevant calculation is intentionally modest; the harder part is deciding which variable belongs in the numerator, denominator, exponent, logarithm, or proportionality.
questions.stem: Which interpretation best explains the main result in the evaporative cooling patch experiment?

choices.A: Evaporation releases heat into the skin model because vapor is less dense than liquid
choices.B: Phase changes cannot transfer heat unless temperature changes at the same time
choices.C: The pattern is most likely random noise despite the systematic dose-response trend and stated controls
choices.D: The cooling is best explained by latent heat absorbed during vaporization of water

explanations.A: This choice is based on a recognizable misconception, but it does not fit the complete experimental design. The claim that Evaporation releases heat into the skin model because vapor is less dense than liquid conflicts with the stated relationship, q = mLv for phase change at constant temperature during vaporization, or with the direction shown in the figure. The passage reports that heat removed increased approximately in proportion to evaporated mass and states that airflow, initial temperature, patch area, and humidity were controlled. Those details make it inappropriate to explain the result by changing a variable that was controlled, reversing the predicted proportionality, or ignoring the units of the measured quantity. A stronger answer must connect the independent variable to the observed trend through the governing C/P principle.
explanations.B: This choice is based on a recognizable misconception, but it does not fit the complete experimental design. The claim that Phase changes cannot transfer heat unless temperature changes at the same time conflicts with the stated relationship, q = mLv for phase change at constant temperature during vaporization, or with the direction shown in the figure. The passage reports that heat removed increased approximately in proportion to evaporated mass and states that airflow, initial temperature, patch area, and humidity were controlled. Those details make it inappropriate to explain the result by changing a variable that was controlled, reversing the predicted proportionality, or ignoring the units of the measured quantity. A stronger answer must connect the independent variable to the observed trend through the governing C/P principle.
explanations.C: This choice is based on a recognizable misconception, but it does not fit the complete experimental design. The claim that The pattern is most likely random noise despite the systematic dose-response trend and stated controls conflicts with the stated relationship, q = mLv for phase change at constant temperature during vaporization, or with the direction shown in the figure. The passage reports that heat removed increased approximately in proportion to evaporated mass and states that airflow, initial temperature, patch area, and humidity were controlled. Those details make it inappropriate to explain the result by changing a variable that was controlled, reversing the predicted proportionality, or ignoring the units of the measured quantity. A stronger answer must connect the independent variable to the observed trend through the governing C/P principle.
explanations.D: Correct. This answer accounts for the manipulation, the plotted trend, and the controlled variables at the same time. The passage identifies the relevant relationship as q = mLv for phase change at constant temperature during vaporization, so the reasoning depends on how the variables are related rather than on a keyword match. In this experiment, heat removed increased approximately in proportion to evaporated mass, while airflow, initial temperature, patch area, and humidity were controlled. That pattern supports this choice because The cooling is best explained by latent heat absorbed during vaporization of water. It also predicts the correct follow-up behavior if the same variable is changed again. The tempting alternatives either reverse the dependency, mistake a controlled variable for the causal variable, treat a nonlinear relationship as linear, or confuse a measured signal with the physical quantity that produced it.

choices.D: The figure shows greater heat removal when more water evaporates under matched airflow and area

questions.stem: Which broader Chemical and Physical Foundations principle is most directly illustrated by this experiment?

choices.A: Quantitative graphs should be interpreted without considering units or controlled variables
choices.B: Phase changes can absorb or release heat without requiring a temperature change during the phase transition
choices.C: A controlled variable is the most likely cause of a trend whenever it appears in an equation
choices.D: A memorized definition should override a measured trend when the two appear inconsistent, even though the passage states that airflow was controlled and the measured trend moved systematically in the figure

questions.passage:
Researchers investigated a spirometer that measured exhaled air volume before and after warming the gas sample to body temperature to connect a measurable physical trend with a the physical sciences principle. The central idea was that for a fixed amount of gas at constant pressure, volume is directly proportional to absolute temperature. Before collecting data, the team identified the governing relationship as PV = nRT, so V1/T1 = V2/T2 when pressure and moles are constant. This mattered because the same topic vocabulary could support several tempting but incompatible answers unless the variables, units, and controlled conditions were kept straight. The investigators therefore designed the experiment so that the figure had to be interpreted through proportionality, limiting behavior, and the direction of causality rather than through sentence recall alone.

The experimental manipulation involved warming the same sealed gas sample from room temperature toward body temperature while pressure was allowed to equalize. Figure 1 summarizes the main result: measured volume increased as absolute temperature increased. The investigators repeated the measurements after verifying that gas amount, pressure equilibration, water vapor correction, and volume calibration were controlled. Those controls were important because a change in a calibration setting, environmental condition, sample geometry, or hidden concentration could create an apparent trend without testing the intended physical relationship. A correct explanation must therefore account for both what was deliberately changed and what was explicitly held constant.

Two alternative interpretations were considered. One was that warming a gas lowers volume because molecules become smaller at higher temperature. Another was that temperature in Celsius can be used directly in gas-law ratios without conversion to Kelvin. Each alternative contains a familiar phrase from chemistry or physics, so it could attract a student who recognizes a term but does not check whether the prediction matches the graph. The investigators rejected these explanations because they either reverse the expected direction, treat a nonlinear relationship as linear, ignore a conserved quantity, or attribute the effect to a controlled variable.

The follow-up analysis asked students to make a prediction from the same relationship. For this passage, the key is to connect the measured trend to spirometry gas warming correction, translate the graph into the appropriate mathematical dependency, and use the controls to rule out artifacts. The relevant calculation is intentionally modest; the harder part is deciding which variable belongs in the numerator, denominator, exponent, logarithm, or proportionality.
questions.stem: Which interpretation best explains the main result in the spirometry gas warming correction experiment?

choices.A: Warming a gas lowers volume because molecules become smaller at higher temperature
choices.B: The volume increase is best explained by Charles-law behavior using absolute temperature
choices.C: Temperature in celsius can be used directly in gas-law ratios without conversion to kelvin
choices.D: The pattern is most likely random noise despite the systematic dose-response trend and stated controls

The passage reports that measured volume increased as absolute temperature increased and states that gas amount, pressure equilibration, water vapor correction, and volume calibration were controlled. Those details make it inappropriate to explain the result by changing a variable that was controlled, reversing the predicted proportionality, or ignoring the units of the measured quantity. The passage reports that measured volume increased as absolute temperature increased and states that gas amount, pressure equilibration, water vapor correction, and volume calibration were controlled. Those details make it inappropriate to explain the result by changing a variable that was controlled, reversing the predicted proportionality, or ignoring the units of the measured quantity. A stronger answer must connect the independent variable to the observed trend through the governing C/P principle.
explanations.C: Correct. This answer accounts for the manipulation, the plotted trend, and the controlled variables at the same time. The passage identifies the relevant relationship as PV = nRT, so V1/T1 = V2/T2 when pressure and moles are constant, so the reasoning depends on how the variables are related rather than on a keyword match. In this experiment, measured volume increased as absolute temperature increased, while gas amount, pressure equilibration, water vapor correction, and volume calibration were controlled. That pattern supports this choice because The pressure equilibration control weakens the claim that the volume change was caused by different external pressure. It also predicts the correct follow-up behavior if the same variable is changed again. The tempting alternatives either reverse the dependency, mistake a controlled variable for the causal variable, treat a nonlinear relationship as linear, or confuse a measured signal with the physical quantity that produced it.

The claim that The graph can be interpreted only by comparing the measured trend with the governing equation conflicts with the stated relationship, PV = nRT, so V1/T1 = V2/T2 when pressure and moles are constant, or with the direction shown in the figure. A stronger answer must connect the independent variable to the observed trend through the governing C/P principle.

questions.stem: Which broader Chemical and Physical Foundations principle is most directly illustrated by this experiment?

choices.A: Quantitative graphs should be interpreted without considering units or controlled variables
choices.B: Ideal-gas reasoning requires absolute temperature and attention to which variables are held constant
choices.C: A controlled variable is the most likely cause of a trend whenever it appears in an equation
choices.D: A memorized definition should override a measured trend when the two appear inconsistent, even though the passage states that gas amount was controlled and the measured trend moved systematically in the figure
